Choice C: Fluconazole is a triazole antifungal that works by inhibiting fungal cytochrome P450-dependent enzyme lanosterol 14-α-demethylase, thereby impairing ergosterol synthesis and disrupting fungal cell membrane integrity. It is commonly prescribed for oropharyngeal candidiasis, esophageal candidiasis, and cryptococcal meningitis. Fluconazole has good oral bioavailability and CNS penetration. Its primary toxicities include hepatotoxicity and QT interval prolongation; it may also inhibit human P450 enzymes, leading to drug interactions. Choice D: Sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim (TMP-SMX) is a synergistic combination of a sulfonamide and a dihydrofolate reductase inhibitor that sequentially blocks folate synthesis in bacteria. It is used as prophylaxis and treatment for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP), urinary tract infections, and Nocardia infections. TMP-SMX is widely used in patients with HIV as prophylaxis against PJP when CD4 counts fall below 200/mm³. Notable adverse effects include rash (sulfa allergy causing Stevens-Johnson syndrome) and bone marrow suppression (neutropenia). Choice E: Zidovudine is a nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI) that is incorporated into viral DNA, leading to chain termination during replication of HIV. It is used in combination ART for HIV, especially in pregnant women and for post-exposure prophylaxis. Zidovudine’s major toxicities include macrocytic anemia, neutropenia, myopathy, and lactic acidosis due to its mitochondrial toxicity. However, its mitochondrial toxicity does not significantly involve the pancreas and it is not known to cause pancreatitis. The drug is more likely to impair hematopoietic and muscle tissues than pancreatic acinar cells. Furthermore, its use has declined due to a relatively high toxicity profile compared to newer NRTIs. In contrast, didanosine has a stronger association with pancreatitis due to its mitochondrial effects on the pancreas. Wrong answer explanations: The correct answer is B. Didanosine is a nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI) used in the treatment of HIV. While it is no longer a first-line agent due to its toxicity profile, it may still be used in resource-limited settings or as part of salvage regimens in cases of multidrug resistance. One of its most important and well-documented toxicities is dose-dependent pancreatitis, which can be life-threatening. Patients with HIV on didanosine therapy presenting with epigastric pain that radiates to the back, nausea, vomiting, and elevated pancreatic enzymes should be suspected of having drug-induced pancreatitis, especially when other causes such as gallstones and alcohol use have been ruled out. Didanosine-induced pancreatitis is thought to occur due to mitochondrial toxicity and disruption of pancreatic acinar cells, leading to autodigestion. Risk is increased with co-administration of other hepatotoxic or pancreatotoxic drugs (like stavudine or hydroxyurea), as well as with alcohol use or underlying hypertriglyceridemia. In this case, the patient has a clear clinical picture of acute pancreatitis, no evidence of gallstones or significant alcohol intake, and recent changes in his ART regimen—making didanosine the most likely offending agent. Management requires immediate discontinuation of didanosine and supportive care for pancreatitis.

stem: Which of the following best describes the underlying mechanism responsible for this patient’s pulmonary findings?

options.A: Alveolar destruction with loss of elastic recoil
options.B: Chronic alveolar inflammation with fibroblast proliferation and collagen deposition
options.C: Deposition of anthracotic pigment and silicon dioxide particles in alveolar macrophages
options.D: Eosinophilic infiltration of lung tissue with release of cytotoxic granules
options.E: Neutrophil-mediated destruction of alveolar septa secondary to anti-protease deficiency

stem: A 42-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his brother after suddenly collapsing while working on a rural farm. Earlier in the day, he had been spraying the crops with a chemical pesticide without wearing protective equipment. According to his brother, the patient began complaining of dizziness, nausea, and difficulty seeing clearly. Within minutes, he developed excessive sweating, abdominal cramps, and vomiting. Shortly thereafter, he became confused, stumbled several times, and had a brief generalized convulsion that resolved spontaneously. Upon arrival at the hospital, the patient is lying on the gurney, drooling excessively and muttering incoherently. His clothes are damp with sweat, and he is visibly trembling. His breathing is shallow, and he intermittently coughs up small amounts of clear sputum. Physical examination reveals pinpoint pupils that respond sluggishly to light. His skin is cool and clammy, and muscle fasciculations are observed in both upper and lower extremities. Bowel sounds are hyperactive, and there is no abdominal distension or tenderness. The patient’s brother says that no one else on the farm has had similar symptoms today. He adds that this particular pesticide had a “strong, bitter” smell, and that the patient accidentally spilled some on his shirt before continuing to work. The patient’s past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. Which of the following best describes the underlying mechanism responsible for this patient’s presentation?

options.E: Increased cholinergic activity
options.F: Increased dopaminergic activity Organophosphate poisoning causes increased cholinergic activity by inhibiting acetylcholinesterase, leading to excessive stimulation of muscarinic and nicotinic receptors. Early recognition and treatment with atropine and pralidoxime are critical to prevent respiratory failure and death. Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY Adverse effects) Bottom line: Choice A: Decreased adrenergic activity would not produce the combination of miosis, bronchorrhea, bradycardia, and hyperactive bowel sounds. Adrenergic blockade could cause hypotension and bradycardia but would not account for marked secretions, muscle fasciculations, or seizures. Moreover, adrenergic pathways primarily mediate sympathetic responses, and suppression would not produce the full “DUMBELS” presentation seen here. Choice B: Decreased cholinergic activity occurs in conditions such as botulism, which inhibits acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction. Such patients present with descending flaccid paralysis, dry mouth, and dilated pupils—not hyperactive bowel sounds or excessive secretions. This patient has the opposite—excessive cholinergic stimulation. Choice C: Decreased serotonergic activity may contribute to depression or certain sleep disorders but has no direct relationship to the acute cholinergic crisis described here. Serotonin deficiency does not cause miosis, bronchorrhea, or neuromuscular hyperactivity. Choice D: Increased adrenergic activity would present with tachycardia, hypertension, mydriasis, and diaphoresis but not bradycardia, miosis, or profuse secretions. Adrenergic excess is seen in stimulant intoxication (eg, cocaine, amphetamines) and differs sharply from this patient’s presentation. Choice F: Increased dopaminergic activity is associated with psychiatric symptoms such as hallucinations and delusions (eg, schizophrenia) or with dopaminergic drug use in Parkinson disease. It does not cause the parasympathetic overstimulation observed here. Wrong answer explanations: The correct answer is E. This patient’s presentation—profuse salivation, diaphoresis, pinpoint pupils, muscle fasciculations, gastrointestinal hyperactivity, respiratory distress, and seizure—suggests acute organophosphate poisoning. Organophosphates (eg, malathion, parathion) inhibit acetylcholinesterase, the enzyme responsible for breaking down acetylcholine in synaptic clefts. Inhibition leads to accumulation of acetylcholine at both muscarinic and nicotinic receptors, causing overstimulation of parasympathetic and neuromuscular junction activity. Muscarinic effects include bronchorrhea, bradycardia, miosis, lacrimation, salivation, and diarrhea, while nicotinic effects include muscle cramps, fasciculations, and weakness. Central nervous system overstimulation can lead to confusion, seizures, and coma. The cholinergic excess seen here is sometimes remembered by the mnemonic “DUMBELS” (Diarrhea, Urination, Miosis, Bronchospasm/bronchorrhea, Emesis, Lacrimation, Salivation). Organophosphate toxicity is a medical emergency requiring prompt administration of atropine, a muscarinic antagonist, to reverse life-threatening parasympathetic overstimulation, followed by pralidoxime to reactivate/regenerate acetylcholinesterase at nicotinic sites. If untreated, patients can develop respiratory failure due to bronchoconstriction, bronchorrhea, and paralysis of the diaphragm and accessory respiratory muscles. This case illustrates that the underlying mechanism is not a deficiency in neurotransmitter release but rather an excess due to enzyme inhibition. Unlike conditions with decreased cholinergic activity (eg, myasthenia gravis), which cause fatigable weakness without parasympathetic overdrive, organophosphate toxicity presents with widespread muscarinic and nicotinic activation. The physiologic hallmark is uncontrolled stimulation of cholinergic receptors throughout the body due to persistent acetylcholine presence.

stem: A 55-year-old postmenopausal woman presents to the clinic for a routine health maintenance examination. She says she has been generally healthy and lives a fairly active lifestyle. She exercises by walking several times per week, maintains a low-fat diet, and has kept her weight stable. She does not drink alcohol and has never smoked. She has no personal history of chronic medical conditions such as diabetes, hypertension, or hyperlipidemia. Approximately three years ago, she began experiencing bothersome hot flashes, night sweats, and difficulty sleeping due to vasomotor instability. At the time, her physician prescribed hormone replacement therapy, consisting of estrogen combined with progestin, which she continues to take daily. She reports that her menopausal symptoms have greatly improved since starting therapy, and she feels much more comfortable in daily life. She denies vaginal bleeding, abdominal pain, bloating, or urinary symptoms. She has never had abnormal Pap smears. She has not experienced breast pain, nipple discharge, or palpable breast lumps. She has never had colonoscopy but is aware that she is due for one. Her family history is significant for her mother being diagnosed with breast cancer at age 60, treated with lumpectomy and radiation. No other relatives are known to have cancer. She is married and has two children. She does not use any other medications besides a daily multivitamin. On review of systems, she denies fatigue, unexplained weight loss, or bone pain. She continues to feel well overall. On examination, her vital signs are normal, with blood pressure of 128/74 mm Hg. BMI is 23 kg/m². Cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams are unremarkable. Breast exam reveals no palpable masses, nipple retraction, or overlying skin changes. There is no lymphadenopathy in the axilla or supraclavicular regions. Pelvic exam shows a normal- sized uterus, no adnexal masses, and no vaginal bleeding. The remainder of the physical exam is within normal limits. Which of the following malignancies is this patient at greatest risk of developing given her current therapy?

options.E: Perineal Combined estrogen-progestin therapy effectively treats vasomotor symptoms of menopause but carries a significant increased risk of breast cancer due to proliferative effects on breast tissue. Progestin protects the endometrium, but both hormones stimulate breast growth, making breast cancer the malignancy of greatest concern in women on this regimen. Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY Adverse effects) Bottom line: Choice B: Cervical cancer is caused primarily by persistent infection with high-risk human papillomavirus (HPV) subtypes, most notably HPV-16 and HPV-18. Other risk factors include early onset of sexual activity, multiple sexual partners, and smoking. Hormone replacement therapy, whether estrogen alone or combined with progestin, is not a risk factor for cervical cancer. In fact, this patient has had consistently normal Pap smears, indicating appropriate screening. While estrogen influences cervical mucus, it does not contribute to neoplastic transformation of cervical epithelial cells. Preventive strategies like HPV vaccination and regular Pap screening remain the cornerstone of cervical cancer risk reduction. Thus, her therapy does not increase cervical cancer risk. Choice C: Unopposed estrogen therapy significantly increases the risk of endometrial hyperplasia and carcinoma in women with an intact uterus, because estrogen promotes unregulated endometrial proliferation. However, in this patient’s case, she is receiving estrogen plus progestin therapy, which protects the endometrium by opposing estrogen’s proliferative effects. Progestin induces secretory transformation and sloughing of the endometrium, preventing hyperplasia. Therefore, her endometrial cancer risk is not elevated, provided she continues combination therapy. If she were taking estrogen-only therapy (such as after hysterectomy), endometrial cancer risk would be a major concern. Choice D: The relationship between HRT and ovarian cancer remains controversial. Some studies have suggested a slight increase in risk with long-term estrogen therapy, but the effect is far less robust than the risk seen for breast cancer. The strongest ovarian cancer risk factors remain genetic (BRCA1/2 mutations, Lynch syndrome) and reproductive (nulliparity, early menarche, late menopause). This patient has no such history described, and her use of combined HRT is not considered a major contributor compared with breast cancer risk. While ovarian cancer screening is not recommended for average-risk women, her management focus should remain on breast surveillance. Choice E: Perineal malignancies (including vulvar and perineal squamous cell carcinoma) are rare. Risk factors include chronic irritation, HPV infection, smoking, and dermatologic conditions such as lichen sclerosus. Estrogen and progestin therapy do not affect the risk of perineal cancers. There is no evidence that HRT promotes neoplastic change in perineal tissues. Therefore, perineal cancer is not a relevant concern in this patient compared with the well-established breast cancer risk associated with combined HRT. Wrong answer explanations: The correct answer is A. Breast cancer risk is increased in women who use combined estrogen-progestin hormone replacement therapy (HRT). Estrogen stimulates proliferation of breast epithelial cells, and progestin enhances ductal and lobular development. While progestin is included in therapy to protect against endometrial hyperplasia, it does not offset estrogen’s proliferative effect on breast tissue. The Women’s Health Initiative and other major trials demonstrated a significantly increased risk of breast cancer with combined HRT, particularly with prolonged use (beyond 3–5 years). The risk is not immediate but accumulates over time. After discontinuing HRT, risk declines and eventually returns toward baseline, but women with family history (such as this patient) have an already elevated background risk that compounds with HRT exposure. Importantly, breast cancer risk rises regardless of dose form — oral, transdermal, or implantable — because the mechanism is systemic hormonal stimulation of breast tissue. For this reason, combined HRT is recommended at the lowest effective dose and for the shortest possible duration to manage vasomotor symptoms. Women who continue therapy require close surveillance with regular mammography and counseling about risks. In this patient’s case, her family history further heightens concern, making breast cancer the malignancy of greatest risk.

stem: A 22-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by police after being found walking barefoot in traffic and shouting incoherently. Bystanders report he had been acting bizarrely, claiming people were trying to kill him. On arrival, the patient is extremely agitated and combative, requiring physical restraints and sedation. His blood pressure is 160/95 mm Hg, heart rate is 112/min, respiratory rate is 20/min, and temperature is 38.1°C (100.6°F). He has significant muscle rigidity, and during the examination, he strikes out at staff and attempts to bite the physician. His pupils are reactive and of normal size. Neurologic exam reveals rotary nystagmus. No needle marks are seen. Which of the following substances is the most likely cause of this patient’s presentation?

options.E: Phencyclidine 4 out of 5 times, PCP is the answer on USMLE for a highly combative patient with nystagmus. A 1 out of 5 alternate presentation is mutism + constricted pupils. Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY Adverse effects) Bottom line: Choice A: Alcohol intoxication causes CNS depression, with features such as slurred speech, ataxia, disinhibition, and eventually somnolence or coma at high levels. It does not cause hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, or violent psychosis, and typically leads to hypotension and decreased reflexes rather than sympathetic overactivity. Choice B: Cocaine is a potent sympathomimetic that causes tachycardia, hypertension, mydriasis (dilated pupils), and paranoia. Although it can cause agitation and even hallucinations, it does not cause rotary nystagmus or dissociation. Cocaine intoxication often includes chest pain, tremor, or seizures, and users remain more alert and goal-directed than PCP users, who are dissociated and psychotic. Choice C: Jimson weed contains atropine-like compounds that are anti-cholinergic, leading to delirium, dry mouth, urinary retention, mydriasis, flushed skin, and hyperthermia. Patients often appear confused, hallucinating, and disoriented but typically lack the muscle rigidity or nystagmus seen with PCP. The USMLE will also give mydriasis (pupillary dilation) with anti-cholinergics. Physostigmine, a cholinesterase inhibitor, is used to reverse Jimson weed toxicity. Shows up on an offline NBME somewhere. Choice D: LSD is a serotonergic hallucinogen that causes perceptual distortions, visual hallucinations, euphoria, and sometimes panic or paranoia. It does not produce nystagmus, violent aggression, or muscle rigidity. Autonomic signs are generally mild compared to PCP. While LSD can cause anxiety or agitation, patients are not typically combative to the degree requiring physical restraint. Wrong answer explanations: The correct answer is E. Phencyclidine (PCP) is a dissociative anesthetic that causes agitation, psychosis, and neuromuscular abnormalities, depending on the dose and patient factors. At moderate to high doses, patients often present with extreme aggression, paranoia, and combativeness, sometimes displaying superhuman strength and resistance to pain, leading to violent outbursts and fighting behavior—as in this case. Nystagmus is often seen. Autonomic instability is common, with hypertension, tachycardia, hyperthermia, and muscle rigidity, increasing the risk of rhabdo. However, PCP can also present with mutism, constricted pupils, blank staring, and catatonia, especially at very high doses—this reflects the dissociative state where the patient appears awake but is disconnected from reality. USME can give this latter presentation sans the bellicosity, and it blindsides sutdents. The diagnosis is clinical, and treatment involves benzos for sedation and supportive care, with observation in the emegency room (benzo + observe in emergency room is a non-specific standard combo for many drug overdoses with agitation).

stem: A 28-year-old man comes to the clinic for evaluation after a routine screening at his local gym revealed an unusually high hemoglobin concentration. He is a long-distance runner who trains intensively for marathons and triathlons, often spending several hours daily in aerobic exercise. He has no significant medical history, does not smoke, and denies recreational drug use. He has noticed some intermittent headaches and flushing of the face during training but denies chest pain, cough, or episodes of syncope. He has never lived at high altitude and has not recently traveled. His appetite is normal, and he has not experienced weight loss, fever, or night sweats. His father has hypertension but no hematologic disease, and his mother is healthy. On examination, the patient appears lean and well conditioned, with normal blood pressure, heart rate, and oxygen saturation at rest. Splenomegaly is not detected, and the rest of the examination is unremarkable. Laboratory results are shown below. Hemoglobin 19.2 g/dL Hematocrit 58% Leukocyte count 7,900/µL Platelet count 250,000/µL Erythropoietin level Low-normal Which of the following best explains this patient’s condition?

options.E: Residence at high altitude This athlete’s elevated hematocrit with low-normal erythropoietin and no hypoxia is due to supplement use. Exogenous erythropoietin or anabolic steroids increase red cell mass, improving endurance but predisposing to dangerous thrombotic events. Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY Adverse effects) Bottom line: Choice A: Chronic hypoxic pulmonary disease produces secondary absolute polycythemia via sustained arterial hypoxemia that upregulates renal erythropoietin, leading to increased red cell mass; typical clues include long smoking history, cough/wheeze, reduced SpO₂, clubbing/cor pulmonale, and polycythemia alongside signs of chronic airway disease on exam or spirometry; in this vignette the athlete has normal oxygen saturation, no pulmonary symptoms, and an erythropoietin level that is not elevated, which argues strongly against a hypoxia-driven mechanism. Choice B: Congenital hemoglobinopathies that cause erythrocytosis (eg, high-affinity hemoglobin variants) shift the O₂-hemoglobin dissociation curve leftward, reduce tissue oxygen delivery, and provoke compensatory erythropoietin elevation; patients often have family histories, lifelong erythrocytosis, normal pulse oximetry despite tissue hypoxia, and persistently high EPO with isolated RBC increases but no consistent leukocytosis or thrombocytosis; the adult-onset presentation without family clues and the low-normal erythropoietin level here are incompatible with a congenital high-affinity hemoglobin process. Choice D: Primary bone marrow disorder (polycythemia vera) is a clonal panmyelosis driven by JAK2 activation, classically showing elevated RBCs plus leukocytosis and/or thrombocytosis, aquagenic pruritus, ruddy cyanosis, splenomegaly, and hyperuricemia; serum erythropoietin is typically suppressed, but PV usually presents in older adults and features tri-lineage proliferation rather than an isolated erythrocytosis with normal platelets and leukocytes; in a 28-year-old endurance athlete lacking pruritus, splenomegaly, or cytoses beyond RBCs, PV is less likely than exogenous performance-enhancing agents. Choice E: Residence at high altitude causes hypobaric hypoxia that increases renal erythropoietin and yields secondary absolute polycythemia after days to weeks, often accompanied by initial low SpO₂, hyperventilation, and adaptation symptoms (eg, headache, sleep disturbance); on return to sea level, EPO falls and hematocrit gradually normalizes; this patient explicitly denies altitude exposure and has a low-normal erythropoietin level with normal oxygenation, which does not fit altitude-induced erythrocytosis. Wrong answer explanations: The correct answer is C. This athletic young man presents with an abnormally elevated hemoglobin and hematocrit in the absence of clinical hypoxia, normal cardiopulmonary examination, and no evidence of residence at high altitude. His erythropoietin level is not elevated, which would typically be expected in secondary polycythemia due to hypoxia or tumors. Instead, the suppressed EPO level suggests that his increased red cell mass is being driven by an exogenous factor, most commonly performance-enhancing supplements such as recombinant erythropoietin or anabolic steroids. Athletes sometimes use these agents to increase oxygen-carrying capacity and endurance, a practice known as “blood doping.” Polycythemia can be broadly classified into three categories: relative polycythemia, primary absolute polycythemia, and secondary absolute polycythemia. Relative polycythemia occurs when plasma volume is reduced (eg, dehydration, diuretic use), leading to hemoconcentration rather than true RBC overproduction. Primary absolute polycythemia is caused by clonal stem cell disorders such as polycythemia vera, in which JAK2 mutations drive uncontrolled proliferation of red cells along with leukocytosis and thrombocytosis. Secondary absolute polycythemia is due to elevated erythropoietin production, which may occur in response to chronic hypoxia (eg, COPD, high altitude, obstructive sleep apnea) or ectopic EPO secretion from tumors (eg, renal cell carcinoma, hepatocellular carcinoma). Exogenous substances like anabolic steroids or recombinant erythropoietin mimic this process, but unlike physiologic secondary causes, they often result in suppressed endogenous EPO levels because of feedback inhibition. The mechanism in this patient involves pharmacologic stimulation of erythropoiesis independent of physiologic demand. Exogenous erythropoietin directly stimulates erythroid progenitors, while anabolic steroids enhance marrow response and increase red blood cell mass. Both elevate hematocrit, improving athletic endurance but significantly raising the risk of hyperviscosity, thrombosis, and cardiovascular complications. Unlike primary marrow disorders, this patient has neither leukocytosis nor thrombocytosis, and unlike secondary hypoxic causes, his oxygenation is normal. Clinically, supplement-induced polycythemia predisposes to venous thromboembolism, myocardial infarction, stroke, and even sudden death during strenuous exertion due to hyperviscosity. Recognition depends on correlating history with laboratory evidence of elevated hematocrit and low-normal EPO levels. Management requires discontinuation of the offending substance and prevention of thrombotic complications.

stem: A 10-month-old boy is brought to the clinic in winter with a 2-day history of fussiness and decreased feeding. His parents mention he has been tugging at his left ear and crying more than usual, especially when lying down. He attends daycare and has had a few colds recently. He was born full-term via vaginal delivery and is up to date on vaccinations. On examination, his temperature is 38.4°C (101.1°F), heart rate is 130/min, respiratory rate is 28/min, blood pressure is 92/58 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation is 98% on room air. Otoscopic exam reveals a red, bulging left tympanic membrane with decreased mobility on pneumatic otoscopy. The right ear appears normal. There is no purulent discharge or mastoid tenderness, and the remainder of the physical exam is unremarkable. What is the most appropriate pharmacologic treatment?

options.E: Levofloxacin
options.F: TMP-SMX The first-line treatment for acute otitis media in children is high-dose amoxicillin, which effectively covers the most common bacterial pathogens: S, pneumoniae, H. influenzae, and M. catarrhalis. Amoxicillin-clavulanate is preferred in children with recent β-lactam use, recurrent infections, or concurrent conjunctivitis (suggesting H. influenzae). For children with a non–type I penicillin allergy, an oral cephalosporin (e.g., cefdinir) is appropriate, while those with a severe penicillin allergy (e.g., anaphylaxis) should receive a macrolide such as azithromycin. Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY use-cases) Bottom line: Choice B: Azithromycin is most commonly used in the treatment of community-acquired pneumonia (especially atypical pathogens like Mycoplasma pneumoniae and Chlamydophila), pharyngitis in penicillin-allergic patients, and certain sexually transmitted infections such as Chlamydia trachomatis. Although it may be considered for otitis media in children with true penicillin allergies, azithromycin is less effective against resistant S. pneumoniae and has inferior efficacy compared to high-dose amoxicillin. Therefore, it is not the first-line treatment for acute otitis media in children without allergies or prior antibiotic exposure. Choice C: Ceftriaxone is a third-generation cephalosporin commonly used for serious systemic infections including bacterial meningitis, gonorrhea, and severe pneumonia. It is also used in children with failed oral antibiotic therapy for otitis media or those unable to tolerate oral medications. However, for an uncomplicated case of AOM in a stable child, an oral regimen such as amoxicillin is more appropriate and less invasive than intramuscular ceftriaxone. Choice D: Clindamycin is most commonly used for anaerobic infections, aspiration pneumonia, skin and soft tissue infections caused by MRSA, and infections involving toxin-producing strains of Strep pyogenes. It may be used in otitis media if there is concern for resistant S. pneumoniae and if the patient is allergic to β-lactams. However, it does not reliably cover H. influenzae or Moraxella catarrhalis, both common causes of AOM, making it an incomplete choice for empiric treatment. Choice E: Levofloxacin, a fluoroquinolone, is used primarily for complicated urinary tract infections, hospital-acquired pneumonia, and Pseudomonas infections. In pediatrics, its use is highly restricted due to concerns over cartilage toxicity and musculoskeletal side effects. It is not recommended for uncomplicated pediatric otitis media and should be reserved for multidrug-resistant infections when other safer options are unavailable. Choice F: TMP-SMX (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) is often used for uncomplicated urinary tract infections, Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP) prophylaxis in immunocompromised patients, and certain skin infections caused by MRSA. However, it has limited efficacy against both S. pneumoniae and H. influenzae due to rising resistance rates and is not recommended for the empiric treatment of acute otitis media in children. Wrong answer explanations: The correct answer is A. Acute otitis media (AOM) is an acute infection of the middle ear space, which lies between the tympanic membrane and the oval window. Infection usually spreads to this space from the nasopharynx via the Eustachian tube, which connects near the torus tubarius. AOM commonly occurs in infants and young children, often following upper respiratory infections. The most frequent bacterial pathogens include Streptococcus pneumoniae and Haemophilus influenzae non-typeable. Children typically present with fever, ear pain, and a bulging, erythematous tympanic membrane, sometimes with visible retrotympanic pus or a middle ear effusion. Infants may show more subtle signs, such as fussiness, reduced feeding, poor sleep, and ear pulling. Amoxicillin is the first-line antibiotic therapy due to its excellent activity against the common pathogens and favorable safety profile. It is a β-lactam antibiotic that covers Gram-positive organisms like S. pneumoniae as well as some Gram-negative organisms including H. influenzae. Its use is standard for uncomplicated cases in children without recent β-lactam use or penicillin allergy. Prompt treatment helps relieve symptoms and prevent complications such as mastoiditis, tympanic membrane rupture, and long-term conductive hearing loss. In patients with risk factors or recurrent infections, amoxicillin-clavulanate may be used as an alternative.

stem: A 37-year-old woman presents to the dermatology clinic with a 3-week history of an intensely itchy rash localized to her left wrist. She reports no prior history of similar rashes but notes that the symptoms began shortly after she began wearing a new fitness watch. She has discontinued using the device 3 days ago, but the rash has persisted. She uses a fragranced lotion on her wrists and recently began using a new body spray. She is otherwise healthy and takes no medications aside from an oral contraceptive. Her vital signs are stable. Physical exam shows a well-demarcated, erythematous, eczematous patch on the medial aspect of the left wrist with lichenification and excoriations. There is no mucosal involvement, and no other lesions are noted elsewhere. Which of the following is most likely to be beneficial for this patient?

options.E: Trial of oral antihistamines Localized allergic contact dermatitis due to a type IV hypersensitivity reaction is best managed with high-potency topical corticosteroids. Immediate allergen avoidance and anti-inflammatory topical therapy are first-line; diagnostic patch testing and systemic treatments are reserved for chronic, refractory, or widespread cases. Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY use-cases) Bottom line: Choice B: Immediate patch testing is not indicated in the acute setting of allergic contact dermatitis. It is typically performed 2–6 weeks after resolution of inflammation to avoid false negatives. Conducting it while the skin is still inflamed may impair the accuracy of results. Identifying triggers is important, but not the first step when the patient is symptomatic. Initial treatment should focus on symptom control and halting the immune response. Choice C: Oral corticosteroids are generally reserved for cases involving extensive skin involvement or for highly symptomatic or refractory disease. For a localized lesion, topical therapy is effective and preferred due to fewer systemic side effects. Systemic corticosteroids carry the risk of adrenal suppression, hyperglycemia, and infection. Their use in uncomplicated, localized contact dermatitis is excessive. High-potency topical steroids are adequate first-line therapy in this patient. Choice D: Topical tacrolimus is a calcineurin inhibitor used primarily for atopic dermatitis, especially in steroid-sensitive areas such as the face and intertriginous regions. It is less effective than corticosteroids in treating thickened lesions or those with significant inflammation. It also does not provide the vasoconstrictive effect needed to reduce erythema and edema. Furthermore, tacrolimus has a slower onset of action and can cause a burning sensation upon application. It is not appropriate for initial management of localized allergic contact dermatitis. Choice E: Oral antihistamines are helpful for symptomatic relief of itching but do not address the underlying T-cell–mediated inflammation. They are useful adjuncts in generalized pruritus or urticaria but are not sufficient as monotherapy in allergic contact dermatitis. They have no effect on skin inflammation, vesiculation, or barrier dysfunction. Sedating antihistamines may aid sleep in itchy patients but do not alter disease course. They are not appropriate as the next best step in management. Wrong answer explanations: The correct answer is A. The next best step in this patient with classic allergic contact dermatitis is the application of high-potency topical corticosteroids e.g clobetasol. These agents work by suppressing local T-cell–mediated inflammation, decreasing cytokine release, and promoting vasoconstriction. This results in rapid symptom control, especially for thickened, lichenified skin on the extremities. Common triggers like nickel (from jewelry or watch straps), fragrance compounds, or preservatives often lead to type IV hypersensitivity reactions, which are best treated by minimizing further antigen exposure and suppressing the T-cell response locally. Usually, mid-potency corticosteroids are used first, then escalated to high-potency ones. In this case, 'mid-potency' is not listed and furthermore, the severity of the condition as depicted by the lichenification and excoriation can justify the usage of high-potency corticosteroids immediately. High-potency topical corticosteroids are appropriate for limited, non-facial lesions. These agents reduce the inflammatory response, relieve pruritus, and prevent further skin barrier damage. Systemic therapy is reserved for widespread or refractory disease. After symptom resolution, patients may require patch testing to identify the specific allergen for future avoidance, but this is not done in the acute phase. Moisturizers may also be recommended to aid skin barrier repair, but they are adjunctive, not primary, treatment.

stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in pharmacologic therapy?

options.E: Sodium bicarbonate

stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.E: Osteosarcoma

stem: A 64-year-old man comes to the clinic for follow-up 6 weeks after undergoing percutaneous coronary intervention with placement of a drug-eluting stent in the proximal left anterior descending artery for unstable angina. He reports no chest pain or dyspnea and is adherent to his dual antiplatelet therapy. His blood pressure and heart rate are well controlled. Review of the stent placement report notes that the device was coated with paclitaxel. Which of the following best describes the primary mechanism of action of the drug in this scenario?

options.A: Activates platelet cAMP to reduce platelet aggregation
options.B: Blocks the conversion of arachidonic acid to thromboxane A₂
options.C: Decreases proliferation of smooth muscle around the stent
options.D: Inhibits the ADP P2Y₁₂ platelet receptor
options.E: Prevents binding of fibrinogen to platelet glycoprotein IIb/IIIa receptors Drug-eluting stents reduce in-stent restenosis by releasing antiproliferative agents such as paclitaxel (microtubule stabilizer, M-phase arrest) or sirolimus (mTOR inhibitor, G₁ arrest) to inhibit vascular smooth muscle proliferation. Both require prolonged dual antiplatelet therapy due to delayed endothelialization and increased late thrombosis risk. Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY use-cases) Bottom line: Choice A: Activating platelet cAMP to reduce platelet aggregation describes the mechanism of dipyridamole or cilostazol, phosphodiesterase inhibitors that increase cAMP levels in platelets and vascular smooth muscle, leading to antiplatelet effects and vasodilation. These drugs are not used as stent coatings to prevent restenosis but rather as oral agents for intermittent claudication or stroke prevention. Choice B: Blocking the conversion of arachidonic acid to thromboxane A₂ describes aspirin’s irreversible cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1) inhibition in platelets. Aspirin is used systemically to reduce thrombosis risk after stent placement but does not directly inhibit smooth muscle proliferation or prevent restenosis when applied to a stent surface. Choice D: Inhibiting the ADP P2Y₁₂ platelet receptor is the mechanism of clopidogrel, prasugrel, and ticagrelor, which prevent platelet activation and aggregation. These drugs are essential components of dual antiplatelet therapy after stent placement but do not directly suppress smooth muscle proliferation in the stented artery wall. Choice E: Preventing fibrinogen from binding to platelet glycoprotein IIb/IIIa receptors is the mechanism of intravenous GP IIb/IIIa inhibitors (e.g., abciximab, eptifibatide, tirofiban). These agents are sometimes used during percutaneous coronary intervention to acutely prevent platelet aggregation but have no effect on the cellular proliferation that drives restenosis. Wrong answer explanations: The correct answer is C. Paclitaxel is an antimicrotubule chemotherapeutic agent that stabilizes microtubules and inhibits their depolymerization, effectively arresting cells in the M phase of the cell cycle. When used as a drug-eluting stent (DES) coating, paclitaxel exerts a cytostatic effect on vascular smooth muscle cells in the arterial wall, reducing their proliferation and migration. This prevents neointimal hyperplasia — the overgrowth of smooth muscle and extracellular matrix that can re-narrow the vessel lumen, a process called in-stent restenosis. Sirolimus (rapamycin), an mTOR inhibitor, is another drug commonly used in DES; it arrests cells in the G₁ phase and inhibits smooth muscle proliferation by blocking IL-2 signal transduction. Both drugs achieve the same end goal of minimizing restenosis, though they act at different points in the cell cycle. After angioplasty and stent placement, endothelial injury triggers platelet activation, inflammation, and smooth muscle cell migration into the intima. Bare-metal stents are particularly prone to restenosis within months. Drug-eluting stents coated with paclitaxel or sirolimus gradually release the drug locally over weeks, reducing this overgrowth without significant systemic toxicity. However, because they delay endothelialization of the stent surface, DES carry a higher risk of late stent thrombosis compared to bare-metal stents, which is why patients must remain on prolonged dual antiplatelet therapy. The choice between paclitaxel- and sirolimus-coated stents is often institution- or operator-dependent, but both share the antiproliferative strategy.

stem: What is the next best step in management?

options.C: Begin IV esmolol followed by nitroprusside
options.E: Start high-dose NSAIDs and colchicine

stem: A 19-year-old college student returns from a summer volunteer trip to coastal Kenya and presents with fever, chills, headache, and malaise that began 2 days ago. She reports taking chloroquine weekly as malaria prophylaxis, starting 2 weeks before her trip and continuing during and after travel. She denies missing any doses. Physical examination reveals a temperature of 39.1 C and mild splenomegaly. Blood smear confirms the presence of Plasmodium falciparum with ring forms visible inside erythrocytes. She is admitted and started on artemether-lumefantrine, with good clinical response. She is concerned that she took all her medications correctly and asks how she became infected. Which of the following best explains her infection despite prophylaxis?

options.B: Inadequate blood levels due to poor compliance
options.C: Infection with hypnozoites not targeted by chloroquine
options.D: Oocyst-stage resistance from mosquito midgut mutation
options.E: Reinfection due to delayed onset of immunity Mefloquine is often used instead of chloroquine for malaria prophylaxis due to high prevalence of chloroquine-resistant malaria. USMLE likes this mechanism over non-compliance as the most likely reason for infection when someone is given prophylaxis. Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY use-cases) Bottom line: Choice B: Although non-compliance is a common cause of prophylaxis failure, this patient specifically reports strict adherence to the chloroquine schedule. She took it before, during, and after travel, consistent with CDC guidelines. There’s no evidence suggesting absorption issues or missed doses. Her infection is better explained by resistance, not compliance failure. Choice C: Hypnozoites are dormant liver stages formed only by P. vivax and P. ovale, not P. falciparum. These can cause relapse weeks to months later, but this patient’s symptoms began just days after returning, pointing to a primary blood-stage infection. Also, chloroquine is effective against blood stages of vivax and ovale—hypnozoites are targeted by primaquine, which is not part of standard prophylaxis. Choice D: Oocyst-stage resistance would involve a mutation in the mosquito vector, not the human parasite, and would not affect drug efficacy in the human host. Chloroquine acts on the erythrocytic stage, not on development within the mosquito. Moreover, there's no established mechanism of malaria prevention failure based on mosquito midgut resistance. This is a distractor and not supported by clinical evidence. Choice E: Reinfection typically requires ongoing exposure to infected mosquitoes, which is unlikely after the patient has returned from travel. Additionally, chloroquine prophylaxis aims to prevent initial infection, not to rely on host immunity, which takes time and repeated exposure to develop. This patient developed symptoms shortly after return, consistent with a primary infection, not reinfection. Wrong answer explanations: The correct answer is A. Chloroquine was once the first-line treatment and prophylactic agent for Plasmodium falciparum, but widespread resistance has developed in many parts of the world, including most of sub-Saharan Africa. P. falciparum may continue to survive and replicate despite appropriate chloroquine dosing, leading to breakthrough infections even in fully compliant travelers. Because of this, chloroquine is no longer recommended for prophylaxis in most falciparum-endemic regions, and alternatives like atovaquone-proguanil, doxycycline, or mefloquine are preferred. Mefloquine is the one you need to be aware of for USMLE as the main chloroquine alternative for prophylaxis. The presence of ring-form trophozoites and the patient's travel to Kenya (a region known for chloroquine resistance) support this diagnosis. Her good compliance and symptom onset shortly after return also align with a primary falciparum infection rather than relapse from P. vivax or P. ovale. Importantly, P. falciparum does not form hypnozoites, so delayed relapse due to dormant liver forms is not a concern here. Treatment requires prompt administration of effective blood-stage antimalarials to prevent complications such as cerebral malaria. This case underscores the importance of matching prophylactic regimens to regional resistance patterns.

stem: A 23-year-old woman is rushed to the emergency department after collapsing at an outdoor gathering. Moments earlier, she developed sudden shortness of breath, dizziness, and generalized itching shortly after eating a dessert containing nuts. Her friends report that she became flushed and started gasping for air before losing consciousness. On arrival, her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 68/42 mm Hg, pulse is 128/min, and respirations are 28/min with audible wheezing. Physical examination reveals diffuse urticaria, periorbital swelling, and use of accessory respiratory muscles. Intramuscular medication is administered immediately, followed by intravenous fluids and supplemental oxygen. Within minutes, her airway obstruction and hypotension improve significantly. The administered drug exerts its life-saving effect primarily through which of the following mechanisms?

options.B: Inhibits histamine release from mast cells
options.E: Vasodilates bronchiolar smooth muscle via nitric oxide Epinephrine treats anaphylaxis by activating α- and β-adrenoreceptors—raising blood pressure, relieving bronchospasm, and preventing further mediator release. Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) Bottom line: Choice A: Blocking muscarinic receptors (e.g., atropine, ipratropium) reduces parasympathetic tone, diminishing secretions and vagally mediated bronchoconstriction but has no effect on the profound vasodilation and capillary leak that define anaphylactic shock; antimuscarinics neither restore vascular tone nor stabilize mast cells, so they cannot rapidly reverse hypotension, airway edema, or mediator surge in IgE-mediated anaphylaxis. Choice B: Inhibiting histamine release from mast cells sounds intuitive, but available emergency agents (H₁/H₂ antihistamines) largely block receptors rather than prevent degranulation, and their onset is slow with minimal impact on airway edema or distributive shock; even cromolyn-like stabilization is preventive, not rescue, so this mechanism cannot address the immediate need for bronchodilation, vasoconstriction, and mast- cell stabilization achieved swiftly by adrenergic activation. Choice D: Suppressing leukotriene synthesis (e.g., zileuton) targets 5-lipoxygenase to reduce LTC₄/LTD₄/LTE₄, mediators that sustain bronchoconstriction and edema in allergic disease, yet these drugs act too slowly for peri-arrest physiology and do not raise systemic vascular resistance or cardiac output; they are adjuncts for chronic asthma control, not first-line life-saving therapy in anaphylaxis where rapid α- and β- adrenergic effects are essential. Choice E: Vasodilating bronchiolar smooth muscle via nitric oxide reflects mechanisms of nitrates and certain inhaled vasodilators that lower afterload and systemic vascular resistance, which would worsen the hypotension and capillary leak of anaphylaxis; while NO can relax airway smooth muscle, it lacks mast-cell stabilization and compromises perfusion, making it counterproductive in a setting requiring prompt vasoconstriction and β₂-mediated bronchodilation. Wrong answer explanations: The correct answer is C. Epinephrine is the cornerstone of anaphylaxis management because it directly addresses all the major life-threatening components of the reaction—airway obstruction, bronchospasm, and vascular collapse. It acts by stimulating both α- and β-adrenoreceptors, which produces complementary physiologic effects that rapidly reverse the pathophysiologic cascade triggered by massive mast cell and basophil degranulation. Activation of α₁-adrenoreceptors causes potent vasoconstriction, increasing systemic vascular resistance and blood pressure while simultaneously reducing vascular permeability in postcapillary venules. This minimizes plasma leakage into tissues, reversing mucosal edema and preventing laryngeal obstruction. α₁ activity also reduces cutaneous flushing and swelling, which are mediated by histamine-induced vasodilation. β₁-adrenoreceptor stimulation increases myocardial contractility and heart rate, augmenting cardiac output and improving perfusion during anaphylactic shock. At the same time, β₂-adrenoreceptor activation produces bronchodilation through smooth muscle relaxation in the airways, thereby relieving bronchospasm and wheezing. β₂ signaling also increases intracellular cyclic AMP in mast cells and basophils, which inhibits further degranulation and release of inflammatory mediators (histamine, leukotrienes, prostaglandins). This secondary effect prevents the progression or recurrence of the reaction even after the initial episode is controlled. The combined α and β stimulation explains why epinephrine is uniquely effective compared with other drugs used in allergic reactions. No other single agent can both constrict dilated vessels and relax constricted bronchioles simultaneously while also suppressing mediator release. Administration by intramuscular injection into the anterolateral thigh (vastus lateralis) achieves rapid absorption due to high vascularity, providing a faster and safer onset than subcutaneous routes. In refractory cases, intravenous epinephrine may be used with careful monitoring due to risk of arrhythmia and severe hypertension. Mechanistically, anaphylaxis results from widespread IgE-mediated mast cell and basophil activation following allergen exposure. Cross-linking of allergen-specific IgE on cell-surface FcεRI receptors triggers exocytosis of preformed mediators such as histamine and tryptase, along with synthesis of new mediators including leukotrienes (LTC₄, LTD₄, LTE₄) and prostaglandin D₂. These substances cause bronchoconstriction, vasodilation, vascular permeability, and tissue edema, leading to airway narrowing and distributive shock. Epinephrine interrupts this cycle by binding adrenergic receptors, stabilizing mast cells, and counteracting both the vascular and respiratory effects.

stem: A 54-year-old man presents with progressive shortness of breath and dry cough over 2 weeks. Medical history is significant for seropositive rheumatoid arthritis, seasonal allergies and newly diagnosed hypertension. He has been taking methotrexate and low-dose prednisone for the past year, and was started a month ago on propranolol and hydrochlorothiazide. He does not smoke and has no history of asthma or recent illness. Physical examination reveals end-inspiratory crackles at the lung bases. Chest x-ray reveals bilateral reticular opacities. Which of the following is the most likely mechanism responsible for this patient's pulmonary findings?

options.A: Alveolar damage due to type III hypersensitivity
options.C: Immune complex deposition in pulmonary vasculature
options.D: Inhibition of surfactant production by pneumocytes
options.E: Methotrexate-induced pulmonary embolism Methotrexate is a dihydrofolate reductase inhibitors used as the first-line DMARD in RA. It causes pulmonary fibrosis, hepatotoxicity, and bone marrow suppression (neutropenia) with mouth ulcers. Reticulonodular/reticular changes on chest x-ray/CT mean honeycombing and are buzzy for pulmonary fibrosis. Leucovorin rescue (folinic acid) can be used to mitigate methotrexate toxicity. Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) Bottom line: Choice A: Type III hypersensitivity is associated with immune complex deposition and complement activation. Though some forms of vasculitis present this way, methotrexate-induced pulmonary changes are more consistent with pulmonary fibrosis. Choice C: Immune complex deposition in vessels can cause vasculitic damage, but it is not the mechanism of methotrexate-induced fibrosis. Immune complex deposition is the pathophysiology of a Type III hypersensitivity reaction (i.e., Choice A), knowing this fact would lead you to excluding both choice A and C, since they are basically the same answer. This is one way the USMLEs can try to trick you, they love to play on the idea of different names for the same thing. Choice D: Surfactant inhibition is not a known effect of methotrexate. This mechanism is more relevant to conditions like neonatal respiratory distress syndrome. Choice E: Methotrexate does not directly cause thromboembolism. Pulmonary embolism would more likely present with acute pleuritic chest pain, tachycardia, and hypoxia. Wrong answer explanations: The correct answer is B. Methotrexate is a folate antagonist that inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, blocking DNA synthesis by preventing the formation of tetrahydrofolate. At low doses, it acts as an immunosuppressant, reducing inflammation in conditions like rheumatoid arthritis and psoriasis. At higher doses, it is used as a chemotherapeutic agent in malignancies such as leukemia, lymphoma, and osteosarcoma. Adverse effects include methotexate- induced lung injury, hepatotoxicity, bone marrow suppression, oral ulcers, alopecia, and teratogenicity. Moreover, as it is a folate antagonist, it requires supplementation with leucovorin (active form of folate). Methotrexate-induced lung injury typically presents as subacute dyspnea, dry cough, restrictive pattern on spirometry and reticular opacities on imaging. The pathogenesis is thought to be immune-mediated, most consistent with interstitial lung disease. Methotrexate-induced pulmonary fibrosis is a non-dose-dependent adverse effect that can occur within weeks to years of therapy initiation. Histologically, it shows interstitial lymphocytic infiltrates, non-necrotizing granulomas, and sometimes eosinophils. This adverse effect requires prompt discontinuation of the drug. In general, when a USMLE question dedicates a significant portion of the vignette on medications, or multiple medications are mentioned, you should keep an open eye for drug adverse effects or drug-drug interactions. Other HY drugs associated with pulmonary fibrosis include amiodarone, bleomycin, cyclophosphamide, and nitrofurantoin.

stem: A 68-year-old woman comes to clinic due to progressive knee pain over the past year. She describes the pain as a dull ache that worsens with walking or prolonged standing and improves with rest. She has no history of trauma or joint swelling. Her medical history includes hypertension and gastroesophageal reflux disease. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Her blood pressure today is 134/78 mmHg, and BMI is 31 kg/m². Physical examination reveals crepitus and decreased range of motion in both knees without effusion or warmth. X-rays show joint space narrowing and osteophyte formation in both knees. She is started on pharmacologic therapy for symptom relief. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of the drug used in this patient?

options.E: Tumor necrosis factor-alpha inhibitor Acetaminophen is the first-line pharmacologic treatment for osteoarthritis because it reduces pain via central COX inhibition without peripheral anti-inflammatory effects. Its safety profile is superior to NSAIDs in non-inflammatory conditions like OA, though overdose can cause hepatotoxicity. Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) Bottom line: Choice B: Competitive inhibitors of dihydrofolate reductase include methotrexate, which blocks folate metabolism by preventing the reduction of dihydrofolate to tetrahydrofolate. This inhibition reduces nucleotide synthesis, impairing DNA replication and cell proliferation. Methotrexate is used in rheumatoid arthritis for its immunosuppressive and anti-inflammatory effects and as a chemotherapeutic agent in malignancies such as leukemia and lymphoma. Choice C: Irreversible COX-1 and COX-2 inhibitors describe aspirin, which acetylates and permanently inactivates cyclooxygenase enzymes, preventing thromboxane A₂ and prostaglandin synthesis. This mechanism underlies aspirin’s antiplatelet effect and its use in cardiovascular disease prevention. Although aspirin has analgesic and antipyretic effects, its risk of gastrointestinal bleeding and nephrotoxicity limits its use for chronic musculoskeletal pain. Osteoarthritis management favors acetaminophen over aspirin because OA is non-inflammatory. Choice D: Selective COX-2 inhibitors such as celecoxib inhibit the COX-2 enzyme involved in prostaglandin synthesis at sites of inflammation, sparing COX-1 which maintains gastric mucosal protection. These drugs reduce pain and inflammation with less risk of gastrointestinal bleeding compared to non-selective NSAIDs. They are used for rheumatoid arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, and osteoarthritis in patients at high risk of gastric ulcers but after acetaminophen has been tried. Choice E: Tumor necrosis factor-alpha inhibitors, including etanercept and infliximab, are biologic agents that bind TNF-alpha to reduce systemic inflammation. They are indicated in autoimmune conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, and inflammatory bowel disease to suppress immune-mediated tissue damage. They have no analgesic effect in degenerative joint diseases such as osteoarthritis because OA lacks an inflammatory pathogenesis. Wrong answer explanations: The correct answer is A. This patient has osteoarthritis, characterized by chronic non-inflammatory joint degeneration with pain worsening on activity and joint space narrowing with osteophyte formation. The first-line pharmacologic treatment for osteoarthritis is acetaminophen, after weight loss as the primary non-pharmacologic intervention. Acetaminophen is preferred over NSAIDs because osteoarthritis is non-inflammatory, and NSAIDs confer unnecessary renal and gastrointestinal risks without additional analgesic benefit. Central-acting COX inhibitor describes acetaminophen’s mechanism. Although it reduces prostaglandin synthesis, it primarily acts in the central nervous system rather than peripherally. Acetaminophen decreases pain and fever but lacks significant anti-inflammatory effects in peripheral tissues, distinguishing it from NSAIDs. For this reason, it is not considered a true NSAID pharmacologically. Acetaminophen is first-line for osteoarthritis pain because it provides analgesia without the nephrotoxicity and gastrointestinal bleeding risk associated with NSAIDs, particularly in elderly patients with comorbidities. Patients with chronic pain who take NSAIDs frequently are at risk of nephropathy and peptic ulcer disease due to systemic COX inhibition and prostaglandin suppression. NSAIDs are reserved for patients with inadequate response to acetaminophen or additional inflammatory symptoms.

stem: A 67-year-old man presents to the clinic for evaluation of chronic lower urinary tract symptoms. He reports a gradually weakening stream, occasional nocturnal urination, and a feeling that he doesn’t fully empty his bladder. He denies pain with urination, blood in the urine, or any recent infections. His medical history includes well-controlled hypertension and hyperlipidemia. He takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. He does not use tobacco or alcohol. Physical examination reveals normal vital signs and a soft, non-distended abdomen. A digital rectal exam shows a symmetrically enlarged, smooth, non- tender prostate. Urinalysis is normal, and serum creatinine is stable. PSA is mildly elevated but age- appropriate. The patient is started on a medication that addresses the underlying hormonal pathophysiology of his condition and can reduce prostate volume over time. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of this medication?

options.E: Stimulate muscarinic cholinergic receptors Finasteride reduces the size of the prostate by inhibiting 5-alpha reductase, the enzyme responsible for converting testosterone to DHT, the main androgen driving prostatic hyperplasia. This hormonal mechanism addresses the root cause of BPH and is used for long-term reduction of prostate volume and symptom control. Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) Bottom line: Choice A: These drugs work by selectively blocking alpha-1 adrenergic receptors in the bladder neck and prostatic urethra, leading to smooth muscle relaxation and improved urinary outflow. Tamsulosin and similar agents are frequently used as first-line therapy for rapid symptom relief in BPH, particularly in men with moderate symptoms and smaller prostate volumes. However, they do not reduce prostate size or target the underlying hormonal stimulation of prostatic tissue. Because they do not affect DHT production, this mechanism does not match the long-term disease-modifying effect provided by finasteride. Choice C: Aromatase inhibitors reduce estrogen levels by inhibiting the conversion of androgens (such as testosterone) into estrogens via the aromatase enzyme. These drugs are primarily used in hormone receptor–positive breast cancer and are occasionally used off-label for gynecomastia in men. However, aromatase has no significant role in DHT synthesis or prostate physiology. Suppressing estrogen does not reduce DHT activity or affect prostatic hyperplasia, so this mechanism does not align with the treatment approach for BPH. Choice D: Phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors, such as tadalafil, enhance nitric oxide–mediated smooth muscle relaxation by increasing intracellular cGMP levels. While approved for erectile dysfunction, tadalafil also offers minor improvement in BPH-related lower urinary tract symptoms. Its benefit is thought to stem from enhanced perfusion and relaxation of the prostatic urethra. However, it does not affect androgen signaling, prostate size, or DHT levels. Therefore, its mechanism is distinct from the hormonal modulation seen with 5-alpha reductase inhibitors. Choice E: Muscarinic receptor stimulation leads to detrusor muscle contraction, enhancing bladder emptying in conditions like atonic bladder or neurogenic dysfunction. However, in men with BPH, stimulating bladder contractility can worsen symptoms like urgency and frequency, especially in the setting of obstruction. These drugs have no role in the pathophysiology of BPH and are not part of standard management. Their mechanism does not affect DHT or prostatic growth. Wrong answer explanations: The correct answer is B. Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) results from androgen-driven proliferation of periurethral stromal and epithelial cells in the transitional zone of the prostate. The principal androgen responsible is dihydrotestosterone (DHT), which is synthesized locally in prostate tissue by the conversion of circulating testosterone via the enzyme 5-alpha reductase. DHT has a significantly higher affinity for androgen receptors than testosterone and is essential for growth and maintenance of the prostate throughout adulthood. During embryogenesis, DHT is also critical for male external genitalia development (penis, scrotum, prostate), while testosterone supports internal reproductive structures such as the vas deferens and seminal vesicles. This dual role explains the importance of both hormones across developmental stages and in adult physiology. Patients with BPH typically present with lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS), including urinary hesitancy, weak stream, nocturia, and incomplete emptying. These symptoms are due to both static (gland enlargement) and dynamic (smooth muscle tone) components of bladder outlet obstruction. On physical exam, a symmetrically enlarged, smooth prostate without nodules is typical. Diagnosis is clinical but often supported with urinalysis to rule out infection or hematuria, serum creatinine to assess renal function, and prostate-specific antigen (PSA) to assess prostate size and screen for malignancy. Ultrasound or post-void residual testing may be used if obstruction is suspected. Management begins with alpha-blockers for rapid symptom relief; however, 5-alpha reductase inhibitors like finasteride are used in men with larger prostates or who are at risk for progression, as they reduce prostate volume over time. Finasteride inhibits type II 5-alpha reductase, the isoenzyme found predominantly in the prostate, leading to decreased DHT production. This reduces androgenic stimulation of prostate tissue, resulting in gradual shrinkage and symptom improvement. Dutasteride, which inhibits both type I and II isoforms, has similar but broader inhibition. It may take 6–12 months to observe significant effects. These drugs lower PSA levels by about 50%, and clinicians must double the measured PSA to accurately interpret cancer risk in patients on therapy. Side effects include erectile dysfunction, decreased libido, and gynecomastia in some men. Beyond its use in BPH, finasteride is also approved for androgenic alopecia (male pattern baldness), where it reduces scalp DHT levels and slows follicular miniaturization that contributes to hair loss. The central role of DHT in external genital development is further illustrated by the rare genetic condition 5-alpha reductase deficiency, particularly of the type II isoform. This autosomal recessive disorder affects individuals with a 46,XY karyotype, who present at birth with ambiguous or female-appearing external genitalia due to insufficient DHT-mediated masculinization in utero. Despite this, testosterone levels and internal male reproductive structures (e.g., testes, vas deferens) remain normal, as testosterone function is preserved. At puberty, rising testosterone levels can partially compensate for the enzyme deficiency, often resulting in virilization, including deepening of the voice, increased muscle mass, acne, and phallic enlargement from the clitoral-like structure. This developmental pathophysiology mirrors the adult therapeutic strategy in BPH, where modulation of DHT—not testosterone—drives changes in external structures and tissue response, reinforcing the tissue-specific, high-potency role of DHT in androgen action.

stem: A 68-year-old woman comes to the clinic for evaluation of progressively worsening urinary symptoms over the past 6 months. She reports sudden urges to urinate that are difficult to suppress, followed by involuntary leakage before she can reach the bathroom. These episodes now occur multiple times daily, and she has begun limiting her fluid intake and avoiding social events out of fear of accidents. She denies dysuria, hematuria, or stress-induced leakage with coughing or sneezing. Her medical history includes hypertension and osteoarthritis, for which she takes lisinopril and acetaminophen. She does not smoke, and her last pelvic exam and urinalysis six months ago were normal. She underwent menopause at age 52 and has never used hormone replacement therapy. On examination, her vitals are normal. Abdominal exam shows no distention or tenderness. Pelvic exam reveals normal external genitalia, well-estrogenized vaginal mucosa, and no prolapse. Post-void residual volume by bladder scan is 20 mL. Urinalysis today is unremarkable. Neurologic exam is normal, with intact reflexes and no sensory deficits. The patient expresses strong interest in a medication to improve her symptoms and quality of life. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: Timed voiding and fluid restriction only Oxybutynin treats urge incontinence by inhibiting involuntary detrusor contractions. It is a muscarinic receptor antagonist (anti-cholinergic). Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) Bottom line: Choice A: Bethanechol is a muscarinic agonist that directly stimulates parasympathetic receptors to enhance detrusor muscle contraction, which promotes bladder emptying. It is primarily used in patients with urinary retention due to detrusor underactivity, such as after anesthesia or in neurologic conditions like diabetic autonomic neuropathy. This patient, however, has urge incontinence, which involves overactive detrusor contractions, not insufficient contraction. Giving bethanechol in this case would worsen urinary urgency and frequency by increasing detrusor muscle stimulation. It is contraindicated in patients with urge incontinence or suspected detrusor overactivity. Choice B: Desmopressin is a synthetic analog of antidiuretic hormone (ADH) that promotes water reabsorption in the renal collecting ducts, reducing urine volume. It is used in central diabetes insipidus and nocturnal enuresis, where the underlying problem is excessive urine production. In contrast, this patient’s symptoms are due to detrusor overactivity, not polyuria or nocturnal volume loss. Desmopressin does not address bladder muscle physiology and can be dangerous in elderly patients, as it increases the risk of hyponatremia and seizures. Choice C: Estrogen vaginal cream Topical estrogen therapy is indicated for atrophic vaginitis, which can contribute to urinary frequency, dysuria, and mild urgency, especially in postmenopausal women. This patient, however, has a well-estrogenized vaginal mucosa and no signs of urogenital atrophy on pelvic exam, making local estrogen therapy unnecessary. Additionally, estrogen cream does not effectively treat urge incontinence or detrusor overactivity directly. Its benefit is limited to improving tissue tone and vascularity in cases of estrogen deficiency. It is not a first- or second-line treatment for true overactive bladder syndrome. Choice E: Timed voiding and fluid restriction only Behavioral strategies such as timed voiding, bladder training, and fluid management are considered first-line for mild cases of urge incontinence or as adjuncts to pharmacologic treatment. However, this patient’s symptoms are moderate to severe, with multiple daily incontinence episodes that are significantly impairing her quality of life. She has already begun modifying her fluid intake, suggesting that behavioral strategies alone are insufficient. Low post-void residual volume confirms this is not overflow incontinence and supports safe use of medication. Therefore, proceeding to pharmacologic treatment is the more appropriate next step in longitudinal management. Wrong answer explanations: The correct answer is D. This patient has urge incontinence, also known as overactive bladder (OAB), characterized by involuntary detrusor contractions that lead to a strong, sudden urge to urinate followed by unintentional leakage. OAB becomes more common with age and is especially prevalent in postmenopausal women. The pathophysiology involves uninhibited detrusor muscle contractions, often due to impaired inhibitory pathways in the central nervous system or changes in bladder afferent signaling. Oxybutynin is an antimuscarinic agent that blocks M3 receptors in the detrusor muscle, reducing involuntary contractions and increasing bladder capacity. This can significantly improve urgency, frequency, and urge incontinence. It is generally well-tolerated, although potential side effects include dry mouth, constipation, and blurry vision. In patients with significant lifestyle impairment and low post-void residuals, pharmacologic therapy is the next best step. Behavioral strategies such as timed voiding and bladder training are first-line, but they can be used in conjunction with pharmacologic therapy or tried first in milder cases. This patient has moderate-to-severe symptoms with lifestyle limitation, making medication an appropriate next step. Her low residual volume rules out overflow, and the absence of infection or prolapse supports a diagnosis of idiopathic detrusor overactivity. In addition to oxybutynin, other agents such as tolterodine (another muscarinic receptor antagonist) or mirabegron (a beta-3 agonist) can be considered. Mirabegron may be preferred in patients with intolerance to anticholinergic effects, but oxybutynin remains a common and effective option for many patients.

stem: A 36-year-old woman comes to the neurology clinic for follow-up regarding multiple sclerosis (MS), which was diagnosed 4 years ago after an episode of optic neuritis and confirmation by MRI. She has been relatively stable on glatiramer acetate and has not had any new lesions or flares in over a year. However, over the past several months, she has developed worsening urinary urgency and frequency. She describes needing to urinate “at least 10 times a day” and often awakens at night with a strong urge to void. On three occasions last month, she was unable to reach the bathroom in time and experienced involuntary urine leakage. She denies dysuria, hematuria, hesitancy, or incomplete emptying. She is sexually active and has no pelvic pain or discharge. Neurologic exam is stable with mild lower extremity spasticity and no new deficits. A urinalysis is unremarkable. Uroflowmetry with postvoid residual shows a bladder volume of 360 mL and postvoid residual of 35 mL. Urodynamic testing reveals detrusor overactivity. She previously tried oxybutynin but discontinued it due to dry mouth and blurry vision. She is eager to find an effective treatment that will not cause intolerable side effects. Which of the following is the next best step in management?

options.E: Topical vaginal estrogen Urge incontinence is common in patients with multiple sclerosis. Oxybutynin, a muscarinic receptor antagonist, and mirabegron, a beta-3 agonist, are common treatments. Baclofen is a GABA-B agonist that treats spasticity in MS. Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) Bottom line: Choice A: Alpha-1 receptor blockers such as tamsulosin or terazosin act on the smooth muscle of the bladder neck and prostate to reduce bladder outlet resistance. These are primarily used in men with benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) who present with obstructive symptoms like hesitancy, weak stream, incomplete emptying, and nocturia. In this female patient, there is no evidence of outflow obstruction, and her postvoid residual volume is normal. In fact, reducing outlet resistance in a patient with detrusor overactivity may worsen incontinence by allowing urine to leak more easily. Therefore, alpha-blockers are not only ineffective but potentially harmful in this context. Choice B: Baclofen is a GABA-B agonist used to treat skeletal muscle spasticity, especially in patients with upper motor neuron disorders like multiple sclerosis. However, it does not affect smooth muscle, such as the detrusor muscle of the bladder. Urge incontinence is primarily driven by involuntary detrusor contractions (i.e., detrusor overactivity), not spasticity of pelvic floor muscles. Furthermore, baclofen can have central nervous system side effects, including sedation and dizziness, which are undesirable in patients trying to maintain independence and functionality. Treating urinary urgency with baclofen reflects a misunderstanding of the underlying physiology. Choice C: Clean intermittent catheterization (CIC) is used in patients with urinary retention, typically due to neurogenic bladder dysfunction where the detrusor cannot contract effectively. These patients present with overflow incontinence, a high postvoid residual (PVR), and often distended bladders on exam. This patient, however, has a PVR of only 35 mL, indicating that she empties her bladder well. Her symptoms of frequency, urgency, and leakage are due to detrusor overactivity, not underactivity. CIC would be unnecessary, uncomfortable, and could predispose her to urinary tract infections without any therapeutic benefit. Choice E: Topical vaginal estrogen is beneficial in postmenopausal women with atrophic vaginitis, where urogenital tissue thinning can contribute to urinary symptoms. It improves tissue quality and local blood flow, reducing symptoms like dryness, irritation, and potentially urgency. However, this patient is a premenopausal woman without any genitourinary atrophy symptoms. Moreover, estrogen therapy does not modulate detrusor muscle function or address the neurologic basis of bladder overactivity. Using it in this case would be inappropriate and ineffective. Wrong answer explanations: The correct answer is D. Mirabegron is a β3-adrenergic receptor agonist that is used in the treatment of overactive bladder due to detrusor overactivity, particularly when antimuscarinic agents (e.g., oxybutynin, solifenacin) are not tolerated. It works by relaxing the detrusor muscle during the storage phase of micturition, thereby increasing bladder capacity and reducing urinary urgency and incontinence episodes. This patient has neurogenic bladder dysfunction from multiple sclerosis, and her symptoms are consistent with urge incontinence caused by detrusor overactivity. Importantly, she has already failed first-line antimuscarinic therapy due to anticholinergic side effects. In such patients, mirabegron is the recommended next step, as it avoids cholinergic blockade and has a better side-effect profile, especially in younger individuals or those susceptible to cognitive effects. Its safety in the setting of normal postvoid residuals also makes it a suitable choice here. Mirabegron has also gained favor in older patients due to its low risk of cognitive impairment, which can be seen with anticholinergics. In MS-related urge incontinence, maintaining neurologic function and minimizing treatment burden are essential, and this therapy aligns with both goals. Additionally, her stable postvoid residual and absence of obstructive symptoms suggest that mirabegron would be both safe and effective in her case.

stem: A 69-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 2-month history of progressive fatigue, shortness of breath with mild exertion, and swelling in his lower legs. He has a history of hypertension, type II diabetes, and myocardial infarction 5 years ago. His medications include lisinopril, metoprolol, furosemide, and a recently added agent for symptom control. On exam, his BP is 102/60 mmHg, HR 54, and he has bibasilar crackles with 1+ pitting edema in both ankles. Laboratory results show serum potassium of 5.8 mEq/L and creatinine of 2.1 mg/dL. ECG shows peaked T waves and prolonged PR interval. Which of the following medications is most likely responsible for his current presentation?
